Well, it was interesting yesterday, the secretary of education, Arne Duncan, also was before the same House Education and Labor Committee, and he said that he has asked the school chiefs of education to report back to him by the end of summer about what they're doing to try to use some alternatives to restraint and seclusion, and that the department wants to help states do that.
And a lot of states have already taken the lead in this.

And the White House has convened a meeting for the first time, bringing educators and people on the Disability Committee together for the first time to try to see what can be done and what steps are next.

First, Secretary of State Condoleezza Rice met with Israeli officials today about ways to end the fighting with Hezbollah guerillas in Lebanon.
She did not ask for a ceasefire, but as civilian casualties in Lebanon grow, so will pressure for a truce.
And many Israelis want to avoid a lengthy ground operation in Lebanon, like the 1982 invasion to stop Palestinian attacks on northern Israel.
The government insists, this time it's different.
NPR's Linda Gradstein reports.
For many Israelis the pictures of Israeli tanks rolling into south Lebanon are familiar.

Israel carried out a limited offensive of its neighbor to the north in 1978 and four years later, Israel launched a large-scale invasion, aimed at destroying the Palestinian Liberation Organizations military capability.
That operation expanded to an Israeli attempt to install a pro-western government that was willing to sign a peace treaty with Israel.
For 18 years Israel occupied south Lebanon, withdrawing only six years ago.
Now Israel is back in Lebanon.
Gerald Steinberg, a political analyst at Bar-llan University, says there are some similarities to the 1982 invasion.
The main similarity is Lebanon is still a very weak country and a harbor for terrorists with a lot of Syrian support.
In '82 it was the PLO and Arafat that was a base for terrorist operations against Israel.
Now, it's Hezbollah, Syria, and Iran.
In both cases, there was also fierce fighting on the ground.
In 1982, tens of thousands of Israeli soldiers were sent into Lebanon.
About 650 Israeli soldiers were killed in the fighting and another 750 during the subsequent occupation.
That makes it Israel's third costliest war, after 1948 and 1973.
Israeli military officials won't say how many soldiers are fighting in Lebanon today but analyst say they believe there are about 3,000.
Israeli troops have taken over several villages near the border.
In 1982 Israeli forces went all the way to Beirut.
But this time, Israeli officials say, the troops will stay close to the border.
Steinberg says Israel's tactics are also different.
The ground operations are only to be used to try to capture those missile launchers, destroy the missile launchers and the storage sites, and not to change the political process in Lebanon.
So the ground operations will go in and go out, they're are not there for a long term basis.
Israel has learned that that doesn't work.

These trainees are on their last day of the course.
They'll spend next week shadowing real staff inside a real Ebola ward before they're certified to work in the new treatment units.
Dr. Murthy says teaching this group how to protect themselves around highly infectious patients is the most important lesson of the course.

Maintaining stringent and powerful protective precautions throughout their time in an Ebola treatment unit is probably the most important tool that we have to fight this outbreak because without health care workers who are motivated to work and without health care workers who aren't scared to work, we're not going to be able to do anything against the combat of the virus and its ongoing spread.
They hope to be able to train several thousand new workers in the coming months.

This is the second group of trainees to go through the course; of the 72 people in the class, 40 of them are health care workers of one sort or another - doctors, nurses, physicians' assistants, even medical students.
The rest are hygienists who will be in charge of cleaning and disinfecting the Ebola wards.
All of them are Liberian, and all of them have to work in head-to-toe protective gear.
As they move through the mock Ebola treatment unit, or ETU, many of them are having problems with their goggles fogging up.
One doctor rips a glove and struggles to pull on another one over the torn latex.
Sweat drips around the edges of their surgical masks.

There's no need for argument.

That shouldn't be an argument.
